title: Pizza margherita
subtitle: The Neapolitan classic, tomato and mozzarella on a blistered crust.
excerpt: A 00-flour dough cold-proofed overnight, stretched thin, topped with crushed tomato, torn mozzarella and fresh basil, cooked as hot and fast as your oven will go. Makes two 30 cm pizzas.
sourceNotes: Pizza margherita, Neapolitan style. The dish is named for Queen Margherita of Savoy; the tricolour topping story dates to 1889 and is associated with pizzaiolo Raffaele Esposito. The Associazione Verace Pizza Napoletana (AVPN) codified the traditional Neapolitan recipe in 1984.

content.text: Home pizza comes down to two things: a cold-proofed dough and an extremely hot oven. The cold proof develops flavour and makes the dough extensible. The hot oven gives the char on the crust edges and the quick melt on the cheese; get it as close to the 485°C of a wood-fired Neapolitan oven as your appliance allows. A pizza steel or stone, preheated for a full 45 minutes, is the home cook's best approximation of a pizza oven floor.
The topping is restrained by design. Fresh mozzarella is torn, not sliced; it should melt into pools with some char at the edges. The basil goes on after baking, not before, heat kills it.

items.name: Tinned tomatoes
items.prepNote: half a tin, crushed by hand; or passata

content.text: Dissolve {{yeast-dried}} of dried yeast in {{water}} of lukewarm water and leave for 5 minutes. Mix {{00-flour}} of 00 flour and {{sea-salt-fine}} of salt in a large bowl. Add the yeast water and {{olive-oil}} of olive oil; mix to a shaggy dough. Turn out and knead for 8 to 10 minutes until smooth and elastic. Divide into 2 balls, place in lightly oiled containers, cover and refrigerate for 12 to 24 hours.

Remove dough from the fridge 1 hour before cooking. Place a pizza steel or an upturned baking tray on the highest oven shelf. Heat the oven to its maximum temperature and leave for 45 minutes. Drain {{mozzarella}} of fresh mozzarella and tear into pieces; leave on kitchen paper to remove excess moisture.

Dust a work surface with flour. Flatten a dough ball with your palm, then stretch gently with your hands, rotating and pulling, until it is about 30 cm across and thin at the centre. Do not use a rolling pin; it degasses the dough. Transfer to a floured pizza peel or the back of a baking tray. Spread {{tinned-tomatoes}} of crushed tomato over the dough leaving a 2 cm border. Scatter the torn mozzarella.

Slide the pizza onto the hot steel or tray. Bake for 6 to 8 minutes until the crust is blistered and the cheese is melted with some golden spots. Remove from the oven. Tear {{basil}} of fresh basil over the top and drizzle with {{extra-virgin-olive-oil}} of extra-virgin olive oil. Serve immediately.

items.fix: Most home ovens need 45 minutes at full heat before the steel is ready. Run the oven on its grill function for the last 5 minutes if it has one.
items.cause: Oven not hot enough, or steel not preheated long enough.

items.fix: Leave it at room temperature for a full hour before stretching. If it still fights, let it rest 10 minutes and try again.
items.cause: Dough too cold, or gluten too tight.
items.symptom: Dough springs back and won't stretch

items.fix: Use a thin layer of sauce. Drain fresh mozzarella thoroughly and pat dry; it releases a lot of water as it cooks.
items.cause: Too much sauce, or mozzarella too wet.

content.text: Naples is the city of pizza, and the margherita is its simplest, most famous version. In the city's pizzerie, a margherita takes about 90 seconds in a wood-fired oven. At home the physics are different but the principle holds: hot surface, fast cook, restrained topping. A Friday night ritual, the week's best meal for the least money.
